toxicity_notes: Raw or undercooked beans contain phytohaemagglutinin (a lectin), toxic and causes GI distress; destroyed by thorough boiling; safe when properly cooked.

Raw/undercooked seeds contain cyanogenic glycosides (linamarin) and phytohaemagglutinin-type lectins, can release toxic cyanide; some wild/unimproved varieties much higher in cyanogenic content; safe when properly cooked.
Raw soybeans contain trypsin inhibitors and lectins that interfere with digestion; must be cooked/processed before consumption; edible and a major food/protein crop when properly prepared.
Generally edible and non-toxic when cooked; raw mature seeds contain modest antinutrients (lectins/protease inhibitors) reduced by cooking; no significant toxicity concern for normal consumption.

Edible when cooked, but contains vicine and convicine which trigger favism (acute hemolytic anemia) in individuals with G6PD deficiency; a genuine and well-documented human health risk distinct from general lectin toxicity.
Generally edible and non-toxic when cooked; raw seeds contain minor antinutrients (trypsin inhibitors/lectins) reduced by cooking; no significant documented toxicity.

Not toxic; edible seed and major global food crop. Not a botanical toxicity, but peanuts are among the most common and severe human food allergens (can cause anaphylaxis in sensitized individuals); also prone to aflatoxin contamination from mold if improperly stored.
Generally edible and non-toxic when cooked; raw seeds contain minor lectins/protease inhibitors reduced by cooking; no significant documented toxicity.
Generally edible and non-toxic when cooked; raw seeds contain minor antinutrients (trypsin inhibitors/lectins) reduced by cooking; no significant documented toxicity.

Generally non-toxic; widely used forage/food crop (sprouts eaten by humans). Documented cautions: raw/sprouted alfalfa seeds contain L-canavanine, linked in some studies to autoimmune symptom flares in susceptible individuals; excessive fresh consumption by livestock can contribute to bloat. Naturalized widely but not generally classified as invasive.

Non-toxic to humans; leaves/flowers occasionally used in food and herbal teas. Contains trace cyanogenic glycosides and can cause bloat in livestock (especially cattle) if overconsumed in fresh/lush form, not a human-toxicity concern. Naturalized very widely but generally not classified as invasive.

Non-toxic to humans; used in herbal teas/supplements (contains phytoestrogens - caution for those on hormone-sensitive conditions or anticoagulants, but not acute toxicity). Can cause bloat/slobbers (from fungal alkaloid contamination) in livestock if overconsumed, not a general human-toxic plant. Naturalized widely but generally not classified as invasive.

Seeds and foliage contain quinolizidine alkaloids (e.g. lupinine, sparteine, anagyrine) that are toxic to livestock (teratogenic, causes 'crooked calf disease' in cattle) and to humans if bitter/wild-type seeds are ingested; 'sweet' low-alkaloid cultivars bred for food use are much lower risk but wild/garden-type plants should be considered toxic.

All parts toxic if ingested, especially seeds and pods (contain wisterin glycoside and lectins); causes nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea in humans and animals. Documented invasive species in the southeastern and mid-Atlantic United States, where it smothers native vegetation and topples trees.

Bark, leaves, and especially seeds/seed pods contain toxic compounds (robin, a toxalbumin, and glycosides); toxic to horses and livestock (causes colic, weakness, cardiac issues) and to humans if ingested in quantity. Documented invasive species in Europe (widely naturalized and classified invasive in many European countries) despite being native to eastern North America.

Generally low toxicity to humans; pollen can trigger hay fever/allergic reactions in sensitive individuals. Some documented negative effects on livestock forage quality/health with heavy browsing, but not acutely poisonous. Documented invasive species in South Africa and Portugal (and elsewhere in the Mediterranean basin), where it displaces native vegetation.

Seed pods and seeds can be mildly toxic/irritating if ingested in quantity (reports of GI upset in children and pets); not highly poisonous but caution advised around large fallen pods. Considered naturalized/locally spreading in some tropical regions but not broadly documented as invasive.
Generally non-toxic; flowers and buds are eaten as food/vegetable in South Asian cuisine and bark/root used in traditional medicine systems; no significant documented toxicity.
Fruit pulp is edible and a major culinary/food crop worldwide; no significant toxicity documented for any commonly used plant part.

Pod pulp has documented purgative/laxative properties (anthraquinone glycosides) used traditionally as a mild laxative and generally shown low acute toxicity in studies; can cause GI upset (diarrhea, cramping) if consumed in excess. Seeds are considered more concerning than the pulp and are not typically eaten.

Leaves have documented laxative/purgative properties (senna genus is a known source of laxative anthraquinones) and can cause GI upset/diarrhea if ingested in quantity; livestock generally avoid it (unpalatable) so poisoning is uncommon. Documented as naturalized and weedy/invasive in many tropical regions (parts of Africa, Pacific islands including Hawaii, tropical Asia).

Contains mimosine, an amino acid documented to cause toxicity (hair loss, reduced growth, thyroid effects) in livestock at high/sustained doses; low acute risk and animals often adapt/detoxify it in the rumen. Documented as naturalized/weedy in many tropical regions and listed as invasive or noxious in parts of the tropics (e.g. Pacific islands, parts of Asia).

Seeds (and to a lesser extent bark/flowers) contain toxic Erythrina alkaloids with curare-like, paralytic effects; documented toxic if ingested, causing nausea, vomiting, and potential nervous system effects; seeds should not be ingested, especially by children or pets.

soil_type: well-drained loam, tolerant of poor soils
toxicity_notes: Low toxicity concern overall; sweet pulp inside pods is edible in small amounts, seeds are hard and not generally toxic, thorns pose a physical (not toxic) hazard. Documented as an invasive species in parts of Argentina (major woody invader) and reported as invading riparian areas in Spain/parts of Europe.

Generally low toxicity; flowers and young pods are reported as edible in some Mediterranean culinary traditions (eaten raw in salads or pickled); no significant documented toxicity for typical exposure.
